description: You are an urban planner investigating whether the 'housing filtering' effect is present in San Francisco's housing market, testing the hypothesis that as buildings age, they become occupied by progressively lower-income households. Using the 'urbansim' kernel in a Jupyter notebook at /home/ga/urbansim_projects/notebooks/filtering_analysis.ipynb, perform an analysis using the 'buildings', 'households', and 'parcels' tables from data/sanfran_public.h5. The analysis involves joining households to buildings and parcels to calculate median household income and residential sales prices by decade_built (floored to the nearest decade), and determining the Pearson correlation between year_built and household income for buildings with year_built > 0. For zones containing both old (pre-1960) and new (1960+) buildings, calculate a filtering index defined as the ratio of median income in old buildings to median income in new buildings. Export the zone-level data (including zone_id, median_income_old, median_income_new, filtering_index, and num_households) to /home/ga/urbansim_projects/output/filtering_by_zone.csv. Create a multi-panel visualization at /home/ga/urbansim_projects/output/filtering_chart.png displaying median income by decade and a histogram of the filtering indices. Document the findings in a summary report at /home/ga/urbansim_projects/output/filtering_report.txt, including the Pearson correlation, the count of zones analyzed, the median filtering index, and a one-sentence conclusion on whether the data supports the filtering hypothesis. All paths are relative to /home/ga/urbansim_projects/.
